metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The numbers of fraudulent and flawed papers accumulate with sickening momentum. Because science runs on trust, that trust becomes its vulnerability rather than its strength. In one case eighteen papers were retracted once the mystery was finally solved. The real question isn't whether fraud exists but why institutions protect the fraudsters.

artifactLines: The numbers of fraudulent and flawed papers accumulate with sickening momentum.
Because science runs on trust, that trust becomes its vulnerability rather than its strength.
In one case eighteen papers were retracted once the mystery was finally solved.
The real question isn't whether fraud exists but why institutions protect the fraudsters.

beats.narration_text: The claim that trust becomes science's vulnerability rather than its strength, alongside eighteen retractions, reframed fraud as a systems failure not a few bad actors. I want to probe the institutional angle. Help me: (1) trace the incentives that make institutions protect fraudsters instead of exposing them; (2) find one real retraction case and follow how long detection actually took; (3) propose a concrete safeguard that would make trust a strength again without paralyzing honest research.
